townAils: The mine is played out and the men aren't leaving
Drought; the wells are down to mud
The railroad is coming through — or past
A feud between the two big families
Too many fresh graves for a town this size
The marshal is a drunk or a coward
Nobody will ride the north road after dark
A revival tent has half the town in it every night
Children have gone missing — two this season
The town is emptying; wagons leave weekly
townSecret: The last shift never came up, and no one went looking
The one sweet well belongs to a man who prays to something for it
The survey line runs through ground the town swore never to open
The stock is drained, not eaten, and the wolves left weeks ago
Both patriarchs answer to the same buried bargain
The congregation set the fire themselves, and won't say why
Fewer bodies in them than headstones over them
He saw what runs this town and was left alive to keep order anyway
Someone feeds the north road, on a schedule, to keep it satisfied
The preacher's sermons are true, word for word, and getting truer
They come back, eventually. Changed. The town pretends not to see.
The ones who leave are never heard from again, and the road east shows no tracks
npcWant: To leave this town and can't
Their child back — from debt, drink, or the dark
To confess something to anyone safe
Protection, and won't say from what
To sell you something you shouldn't buy
To keep a promise made to the dead
What the dark promised them — almost paid off now
npcTell: Counts things — coins, exits, your party
Wears something of the child's, worried smooth
Smiles only when someone else is hurting
Salt in every pocket; checks windows at dusk
Talks too fast, brings written proof no one reads
Overly helpful with directions out of town
Visits the boneyard daily, same hour
The animals won't come near; they've stopped noticing
rumors: The stage hasn't come in two weeks; the road's fine
The new preacher don't cast a shadow at evening service
Old Krieg pays double for night work at his place; nobody goes twice
They hanged the wrong man in '81 and the right one still buys drinks here
A white bison was seen on the flats; the tribes have gone quiet about it
The mine took on a new vein; the ore hums if you hold it
A whole wagon train wintered at the pass and never came down
The Vane girl came back from the river three days after she drowned
There's a gunfighter in the territory can't be killed; ask at the faro table
Cattle on the north range born wrong this spring — every one
The Army's buying salt by the wagonload and won't say why
A woman weeps along the creek at night; don't answer her
Doc's been ordering twice the laudanum for half the patients
The bounty on the Sorrel Gang doubled; the last posse's horses came home alone
Something's been opening graves — from underneath
There's a door in the mesa the old people painted shut
The railroad man buys land nobody'd want at prices nobody'd refuse
The marshal burned a bundle of letters the night the judge died
A traveling show is a day behind you on the road; folk vanish where it plays
It's quiet lately. Too quiet. Even the coyotes have moved on.
trailDay: Weather turning — a wall of it, hours off
Tracks crossing yours: shod horses, moving fast, one bleeding
A homestead: door open, table set, nobody
Fence line cut — from the outside
A stranger afoot, no water, won't say from where
Fresh grave by the trail, marker turned face-down
Good water, good grass — and old bones in the reeds
A drummer's wagon, wares half-price, seller in a hurry
Smoke ahead — too much for a campfire, too little for a town
The shortcut everyone knows about; nobody's used it in a year
A rider pacing you on the far ridge all afternoon
trailNight: The fire won't take, and the wood is dry
A light on the ridge that answers when you cover yours
The horses wake you, staring at the same nothing
Singing, far off, in a language nobody owns to knowing
One of the party talks in their sleep, answering questions
Something big circles the camp once, upwind, and leaves
The stars are wrong over one quarter of the sky
A child's voice asks to share the fire. There is no child.
Dawn is an hour late. Nobody's watch agrees.
Every snare and picket is undone, gently, from within
Frost, in August, in the shape of footprints
You wake where you camped. The ashes are a week cold.
plunder: $2d20 in mixed coin, some of it minted nowhere you know
A good horse, saddled, patient, waiting — branded to a dead man's outfit
A fine revolver with eleven notches and a name scratched out
Letters home, unsent, that explain everything if read in order
A doctor's bag, complete, monogrammed, one instrument missing
A claim deed, legal and current, to ground the locals won't name
A silver crucifix, heavy, bent nearly double by hand
Tintypes of strangers — and on a second look, one of a party member
A journal, sane for forty pages, then diagrams
Trade goods: powder, salt, coffee — worth $30 to the right town, questions to the wrong one
A child's doll, new, dry, forty miles from anywhere
A key of black iron, cold in any weather; you'll know the lock when you see it
omens: The birds stop, all at once, mid-phrase
A rocking chair, rocking, wind or no
Milk gone sour in the pail, morning-fresh
The dog won't cross a particular floorboard
Every clock in the house stopped at the same minute
Fresh flowers on a grave nobody tends
A cold spot the width of a doorway, in open ground
The well rope is wet. The bucket is dry.
Handprints on the window. Outside. Second floor.
A hymn hummed from the barn; the barn is empty
The horses are watching something walk the fence line
Salt spilled across every sill, from inside
Seven crows on the wire, facing the house
The family Bible open to a page that's been cut out
A smell of lightning on a clear day
Your reflection is a half-second behind in the shaving glass
Wet bootprints from the creek to the door, none returning
The baby laughs at the empty corner, every evening, same time
The candle flames all lean the same direction; there is no draft
Somewhere out on the flats, a church bell. There is no church.

The Bruja (III) — the night roads are hers

The Tallyman (III) — a debt come due
The Crossroads Man (IV) — somebody dealt, and lost

The Hornet Swarm (I) — and the horses
The Elk Bull (II) — in the rut
The Stock-Killer Wolf (III) — a whole season's work

The Hired Gun (II) — an agency now
The Resurrectionist (II) — with a price list
The Lynch Mob (II) — a whole ward of it
Dark Cultist & the Hollow Prophet (II) — chartered
The Skin-Walker (III) — a new face each month
The Tallyman (III) — he holds the paper
The Thing in the Well (III) — the waterworks

cityWrongNote: The potter's field is full again
The same charity buries them all

The night shift will not go below
A fine new building on bad ground
The asylum is taking in strangers
The police do not patrol one street
The tunnels were bricked last spring
cityMachine: The ward boss and his saloon
The vigilance committee that never disbanded
The madam who holds everyone's secrets
A benevolent association with a fine brass plate
Nobody, and that is the emergency

Recover a body before the college does

Find who is buying the fresh graves

Attend a lodge dinner and listen
cityQuarter: The stockyards and the killing floors
The shanties by the rail yards
The good street, six years old
The tunnels and the buried creek
